title: Today's Tech Brief: OpenAI's agent chained decade-old DoS attacks to crash web servers in seconds
summary: OpenAI's Codex AI agent used decade-old DoS techniques to crash web servers quickly.

imageSourceTitle: OpenAI's agent chained decade-old DoS attacks to crash web servers in seconds

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and I'm looking at some fresh signals in the tech world. Today, we're talking about OpenAI's Codex AI agent and its surprising ability to chain decade-old DoS attacks to crash web servers in seconds. This caught my attention because it shows how AI can be used in both amazing and unsettling ways.

According to The Register, OpenAI's Codex agent was able to use old DoS techniques to create a new threat. This is interesting because it highlights the ongoing challenge of keeping up with evolving security threats. The agent was able to create an HTTP/2 bomb, which caused web servers to crash quickly. I'm curious to see how security teams will respond to this kind of threat.

The HTTP/2 bomb is a clever name for this threat. Essentially, it's a way to flood a web server with requests, causing it to become overwhelmed and crash. What's surprising here is that this technique is decades old, but the Codex agent was able to use it in a new way to create a more potent threat. It's a bit like finding out an old recipe can be used to make a new, super-powerful dish.

OpenAI's agent chained decade-old DoS attacks to crash web servers in seconds is also worth a quick mention. OpenAI's Codex agent helped uncover a remote denial-of-service (DoS) exploit that can be launched from a sin... The point is not to force it into a giant theory; it is simply part of today's news mix.

My quick read is that today's set of stories is useful because it gives us texture, not a sermon. Each item has its own little job: one tells us what changed, another tells us what people are worried about, and another gives the day its mood.
So that is the brief version: a few headlines, a little context, and just enough connective tissue to make the day easier to follow without turning the whole thing into homework.
